title: SAGA Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: The SAGA Act stops states and cities from making stricter rules about rifles and shotguns than the federal government has. This means local governments cannot charge higher taxes or fees on these guns or create tougher penalties than federal law allows. If states or cities already have stricter gun laws for rifles and shotguns, those laws would no longer work under this bill. The law is meant to make sure federal rules are the strongest rules that can apply to rifles and shotguns anywhere in the country.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Second Amendment Guarantee Act or the SAGA Act.

sections.title: Section 2. Limitation on Authority of States and Localities to Regulate Conduct in Relation to Rifles or Shotguns
sections.text: This section changes an existing federal law about firearms. It adds new rules that limit what state and local governments can do regarding rifles and shotguns. States and cities cannot make laws about rifles or shotguns that are stricter than federal law. This includes rules about how rifles and shotguns are designed, made, brought into the country, sold, transferred from one person to another, owned, or marked. States and cities also cannot charge higher penalties, taxes, fees, or other charges for rifles and shotguns than what federal law allows. This rule applies to rifles and shotguns that have been shipped between states or between countries, or any actions that affect trade between states or countries. If any state or local law violates these new rules, that law will have no power and cannot be enforced. This applies to laws that were made before this new rule, on the same day, or after this new rule takes effect. For this law, rifle or shotgun means the whole gun, any parts of the gun, any removable magazines or devices that hold ammunition, and any type of pistol grip or shoulder stock design. If someone sues because a state or local government violated these rules and wins the case, the court must make the government pay the winner's legal fees along with any other money or relief awarded.
